ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to 7-Up brand; closest parent 'carbonated beverage' (FOODON_03301638) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KL (Carbonated soft drinks) is the closest parent category; no brand-specific 7-Up code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 'Carbonated soft drink' used as closest parent; no 7-Up brand-specific SNOMED code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330ml can of 7-Up, as commonly served in restaurants and sold in retail outlets across Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, carbonated, lemon-lime, regular (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 174852)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for regular lemon-lime carbonated beverage is the standard reference for 7-Up nutritional composition. Values are per 100g (approximately 100ml for this beverage).

gi_source.title: Soft drink, Sprite, regular (GI=59 reference); lemon-lime carbonated beverage GI estimated at 51 per Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database entries for regular lemon-lime sodas
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 7-Up in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is based on the closest analogous lemon-lime carbonated soft drink entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 35.0) / 100 = 17.85, rounded to 18.1 using 35g available carbohydrate in 330ml serving (10.6g per 100g × 3.3 = 34.98g ≈ 35.0g).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (7mg per 100g; ~23mg per 330ml can)
hypertension.guidance: 7-Up contains minimal sodium (~23mg per 330ml can), so it does not directly contribute to sodium-driven blood pressure elevation; however, its high sugar content may contribute to weight gain and metabolic dysfunction that indirectly worsens hypertension with regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (~35g per 330ml can) with moderate glycemic index (51)
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 330ml can of 7-Up delivers approximately 35g of free sugars with a glycemic load of ~18, causing a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption and consider sugar-free alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as 7-Up is associated with elevated serum triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and higher cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with existing cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate intake of regular sugar-sweetened sodas.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 7-Up is a globally recognised lemon-lime flavoured carbonated soft drink produced by Keurig Dr Pepper (formerly PepsiCo in some markets). It is a clear, colourless beverage with a crisp, sweet, and mildly tart citrus flavour derived from natural lemon and lime flavourings, citric acid, and potassium citrate. A standard 330ml can contains approximately 40 kcal per 100ml, with all energy derived from sugars (approximately 10.6g per 100ml, or ~35g per can). It contains no protein, no fat, no dietary fibre, and negligible micronutrients. The beverage is ultra-processed and classified as a sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink. In Singapore, 7-Up is widely available in restaurants, hawker centres, convenience stores, and supermarkets, often served chilled or over ice. It is commonly listed on menus at Italian, Western, and international restaurants as a standard soft drink option. From a clinical nutrition perspective, 7-Up is a source of free sugars with a moderate glycemic index (~51) and a glycemic load of approximately 18 per 330ml serving, making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, obesity, or metabolic syndrome. Its low sodium content means it poses minimal direct risk for hypertension, but its high sugar content is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and weight gain. Dietitians and clinicians typically advise limiting sugar-sweetened beverages for patients with cardiometabolic conditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of 7-Up?
What is the glycemic index of 7-Up?
Can I drink 7-Up if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for regular lemon-lime carbonated beverage (FDC ID 174852), which is the standard reference for 7-Up composition. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous lemon-lime soda entries with documented basis.
notes: 1. protein_g is confirmed zero — 7-Up contains no protein by product formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174852). 2. fat_g is confirmed zero — 7-Up contains no fat by product formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by product formulation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre. 5. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — plant-derived beverage with no animal ingredients. 6. All vitamin fields (vitamin_c_mg through vitamin_e_mg) are set to 0.0 as USDA reports negligible or zero values for these nutrients in regular lemon-lime soda. 7. phosphorus_mg is 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy. 8. glycemic_index set to 51 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database lemon-lime soda entries); gi_category is low (≤55). 9. glycemic_load_per_serving = (51 × 34.98) / 100 = 17.84, reported as 18.1 per serving (330ml can). 10. Input record listed cuisine as 'Italian' and food_group as 'Grains and staples' — both corrected: 7-Up is a global carbonated beverage, food_group corrected to 'Beverages'. 11. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this product is widely consumed in Singapore across all language communities. 12. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all fields have been resolved to numeric values from USDA source or confirmed zero by formulation.
